behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: The risk is in module handoff (directory discovery -> filename parsing -> sort -> execution loop), so unit tests on sorting alone can miss wiring bugs (MOCK-MISS). No real OS/network/subprocess requirement is explicit, and no AI dependency exists. Rejected System because the behavior text only requires ordering by prefix, not a real external contract/error shape/round-trip side-effect proof.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp `migrations/` directory with intentionally unsorted numeric prefixes, run the runner with a test DB adapter that records execution order, and assert executed versions are strictly ascending by parsed numeric prefix.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This is idempotency across runs via `_migrations` state handoff, which is beyond a single function (MOCK-MISS). A multi-module test with real persistence in a local test DB/FS is needed. Rejected System because behavior text does not require real external API contract validation; it requires correct local state coordination across runs.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner twice against the same test database state; assert first run applies migration and records it, and second run executes no migration SQL and leaves `_migrations` unchanged except original entries.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Rollback semantics and stop-on-failure depend on real PostgreSQL transaction behavior and externally visible DB state after failure (REAL-INFRA + SYSTEM TRIAD round-trip side-effect proof). Mocks can hide transaction/driver edge cases (MOCK-HIDE). Rejected Integration because behavior explicitly requires automatic rollback of real statement effects, which must be validated by real DB readback.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real PostgreSQL instance, run migrations where one file contains a failing statement after a write; verify via SQL readback that writes from the failed migration are absent (rolled back) and that later migration files were not applied in that run.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly requires concurrent separate processes plus shared DB coordination, which is real subprocess/OS scheduling + real DB locking/constraints (REAL-INFRA). In-process mocks cannot faithfully reproduce race timing and cross-process guarantees (MOCK-HIDE).
behaviors.test_description: Start two runner processes simultaneously against the same real PostgreSQL database and same migration set; after both finish, assert each migration’s side effects exist exactly once and `_migrations` has one row per version (no duplicates, no misses).

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is initialization ordering across components (bootstrap metadata -> apply files), so unit-only checks are insufficient (MOCK-MISS). It does not explicitly require real external contract/error-path verification; a local integration harness can validate call/order and persisted state. Rejected System because behavior text is about internal sequencing, not real infra-specific failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner on a fresh test database state with migrations present; assert `_migrations` is created and available before migration application logic proceeds, and that first migration then applies successfully with its version recorded.
